issue_title: Reset stop conditions
issue_body:
Assume we have next function:

```python3
@retry(stop=stop_after_attempt(3))
def test_function():
    # while loop that does jobs
    # every successful iteration is a completed job
    while not stop_event.is_set():
        # some code that can raise exception
```

Here we have counter that will decrease on every failed job. But due to while loop it becomes a counter of failed jobs instead of attempt counter.
Although I could separate the job completion code into another function, there's another problem: every next job need data from some previous jobs. So I will need to use outer data, what is slightly non-pythonic, I believe.

Proposal is to add functions to reset stop conditions (all and/or specific).

issue_title: Defining unit with "pi" leading to errors
issue_body:
I am trying to specify offset units for representing and converting from True and Magnetic North, given a magnetic declination, i.e.  `deg_Mag = deg_True - declination`. In doing so, I think I may have come across a bug when using `pi` in a unit definition. Below I create two unit definitions, one using `pi` and the other using the numerical value for pi. Both definitions are able to convert from deg_Mag to deg_True, but when going from deg_True to deg_Mag, the unit defined using `pi` is off by a scale factor of 1/pi. 

```python
import math
import pint
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry(autoconvert_offset_to_baseunit = True)

ureg.define('rad_True = radian')
ureg.define('deg_True = degree')

declination = (10. * ureg.degree).to(ureg.radian)

unitstr_degMag = f"deg_Mag = pi / 180 * radian; offset: {declination.magnitude} = degmag;"
unitstr_degMag2 = f"deg_Mag2 = {math.pi} / 180 * radian; offset: {declination.magnitude} = degmag2;"

ureg.define(unitstr_degMag)
ureg.define(unitstr_degMag2)

print((0 * ureg.deg_Mag).to(ureg.deg_True)) # Expect 10 deg_True, prints 10.0 deg_True
print((0 * ureg.deg_True).to(ureg.deg_Mag)) # Expect -10 deg_Mag, prints -31.41592653589793 deg_Mag

print((0 * ureg.deg_Mag2).to(ureg.deg_True)) # Expect 10 deg_True, prints 10.0 deg_True
print((0 * ureg.deg_True).to(ureg.deg_Mag2))  # Expect -10 deg_Mag2, prints -10.0 deg_Mag2
```

issue_title: Different alignments returned with cigar string is requested
issue_body:
Hello, I was playing around with some test data to see the different output options for minimap2 and I found something a bit off. Essentially, I get different alignment starts and stops when I run the program with the cigar string requested (option -c) compared to when I do not.

So for the same input data,
"tools/minimap/minimap2-2.7_x64-linux/minimap2 -x map-pb ref.fa query.fa"
gets me:
tempread        1797    83      1599    +       dummySeq1       3372    1006    2380    465     1516    58 etc.

while
"tools/minimap/minimap2-2.7_x64-linux/minimap2 -x map-pb -c ref.fa query.fa"
gets me:
tempread        1797    18      1607    +       dummySeq1       3372    947     2388    1384    1620    55 etc.

Asking for a sam-format alignment (-a) gives the same alignment as the alignment with cigar string (-c).

Why is there a different alignment when different output fields are requested? Is one more accurate than another?

ref sequence:
>dummySeq1


query sequence:
>tempread


issue_title: pepper8 does not output HTML errors with the flake8-commas plugin
issue_body:
I have found a bug in the latest version of `pepper8`

1. Install flake8-commas via `pip install`.
2. Enable the errors by including C812 in a setup.cfg file, or with `--select`.
3. Run `flake8` to get text output.
4. Run `pepper8` on the output of `flake8`.

You will be able to see the standard `flake8` warnings and errors in the HTML output, but not the output resulting from plugins, which are present in the text output.

issue_title: Incorrectly sending a comm message
issue_body:
In https://github.com/arose/nglview/blob/4da7c0236994c84bf65dfcd0d6020b5060de6d4f/js/src/widget_ngl.js#L187, the model.send function is used without supplying necessary callbacks, as mentioned in the `model.send` documentation: https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/master/jupyter-js-widgets/src/widget.ts#L126

The usual convention is to call the *view* send method (as is done in other places in the code), which automatically supplies the necessary callbacks. So replace the `this.model.send(...` in the line above with `this.send(...`

Not supplying these callbacks means that the pending message counter in the widget is not updated correctly (it is not decremented when this message is finished being processed), which blocks further sync messages from being sent.

This fixes the issue brought up in https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/issues/1375

issue_title: Ticket form property on Ticket
issue_body:
Great library.

I'd like to set a ticket form id when I create a ticket. Doesn't look like this is currently possible. Looking at https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/core/tickets, I think it's just: ticket_form_id.

issue_title: Doc for masking on Null
issue_body: Hello, in your README examples would be very useful to have how to mask on Null / None, and not null?

issue_title: Only handle the first "=" when import env file
issue_body:
If an environment variable's value include "=", j2cli can't handle it correctly. 

This line: 
https://github.com/kolypto/j2cli/blob/master/j2cli/context.py#L113

better be changed to:
```python
line.split('=', 1)
```

issue_title: ZeroDivisionError: float division by zero
issue_body:
Encountered a new error while running a scaffold job yesterday. Here's the setup:

```

issue_title: Keyring access control in OS X
issue_body:
When a new password is created through the OS X backend it's done through the `security` command, and the new keyring item is therefore set to always allow access from the `security` command. It seems reasonable to me that the creating process should have access, but because keyring delegates to the security command there's a particular problem: `security` will happily dump the password you're saving for anyone else! This access can be manually revoked, at the cost of manually confirming access each run, but there's no way to connect access to your particular application. (I'm not sure how it's verified anyways, so maybe this is a moot point without a signed binary or something of that sort.)

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/144
- Originally reported by: Sam Birch
- Originally created at: 2014-04-29T03:18:19.853

issue_title: `SizeMixin` is broken
issue_body:
Probably an oversight somewhere, but tkinter doesn't expose common attributes as properties of the widgets, i.e. instead of `tk.Tk.width`, provides `tk.Tk.winfo_width()` (whoever thought that was a good idea, but alas). Someone fell for this while writing [the `SizeMixin` class](https://github.com/lawsie/guizero/blob/dev/guizero/tkmixins.py#L143):

```python
>>> import guizero as gz
>>> import tkinter as tk
>>> 
>>> class Widget(gz.tkmixins.SizeMixin):
...     def __init__(self):
...         self.tk = tk.Tk()
...
>>> w = Widget()
>>> w.width
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/guizero/tkmixins.py", line 97, in width
    return self.tk.width
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/tkinter/__init__.py", line 2095, in __getattr__
    return getattr(self.tk, attr)

issue_title: [Feature request] Continuous integration support
issue_body:
### 1. Request

It would be nice, if pyroma will support continuous integration services as [**Travis CI**](https://travis-ci.org/) and [**AppVeyor**](https://www.appveyor.com/).

### 2. Argumentation

Error reporting in `setup.py` and/or `setup.cfg` in continuous integration process.

### 3. Example

my example `.travis-yml` file:

```yaml
language: python
python:
  - "3.6.4"
install:
  - pip install pyroma
script:
  - pyroma .
```

**If** `Final rating: 10/10`:

    build passed.

**else**:

    build failed.

Thanks.

issue_title: Look for the configuration files of pycodestyle and flake8
issue_body:
autopep8 is only looking for the configuration files of pep8, it should also look for the configuration files of pycodestyle and flake8. Like `.flake8` or `.config/flake8`.

It currently only looks for the sections of pycodestyle and flake8, but not the files, which means it will only find them in `setup.cfg`.

This behavior should also be documented clearly.

issue_title: Add test for memory_maps() which loads a library by path
issue_body:
Perhaps this is a bit tricky. Anyway, `Process.memory_maps()` lists the `.so` libraries loaded by the process. It would be nice to add a test that:

* copies an existent loaded `.so` library somewhere (another path)
* load the copied library with ctypes
* call `memory_maps()` and make sure newly loaded library appears in the list

This would pave the way for testing how `memory_maps` behave with unicode paths (see: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/issues/1040).

Some refs:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2327344/ctypes-loading-a-c-shared-library-that-has-dependencies
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7586504/python-accessing-dll-using-ctypes

Collecting psutil

        main()

    AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'replace'

```

When using [PYTHONOPTIMIZE=2](https://docs.python.org/3/using/cmdline.html#envvar-PYTHONOPTIMIZE), [`__doc__` are removed from the generated `*.pyo` files](https://docs.python.org/3/using/cmdline.html#cmdoption-OO), assuming that in a production deployment you never need the docstrings.

Could you please use an alternative method to get that docstring and make it work with maximum optimization?

Thanks!

issue_body:

Different users use different extensions and in different ways and currently nbstripout doesn't strip that data, causing conflicts or/and unnecessary commit noise.

Examples: 

a) if I use ToC extension, but others don't, I end up with:

    -   "toc_position": {},
    +   "toc_position": {
    +    "height": "calc(100% - 180px)",
    +    "left": "10px",
    +    "top": "150px",
    +    "width": "290.391px"
    +   },
        "toc_section_display": "block",
    -   "toc_window_display": false
    +   "toc_window_display": true

this is a  "toc": entry. Perhaps there is a known set of jupyter core top-level entries that can be kept and then all the extension top-level entries removed during stripout?

b) If I use Collapse Headers extension - it adds a bunch of metadata noise:

       {
        "cell_type": "markdown",
    -   "metadata": {},
    +   "metadata": {
    +    "heading_collapsed": true
    +   },
        "source": [
         "# Fin"
        ]
    @@ -1154,7 +1310,9 @@
       {
        "cell_type": "code",
        "execution_count": null,
    -   "metadata": {},
    +   "metadata": {
    +    "hidden": true
    +   },
        "outputs": [],
        "source": []

Perhaps there can be an option to force to set metadata to {}? Which would solve this particular extension.

Thank you!

issue_title: send file with non-ACSII filename
issue_body:
Error will occur when using sendDocument/sendAudio/...etc with non-ASCII filename.
The underlying cause is that the function **format_header_param**() in "requests/packages/urllib3/fields.py" (from library **requests**) is using an old way "RFC 2231" to handle non-ASCII character for the content-disposition of multipart/form-data. However, telegram bot API seem aborted this way and accept the utf-8 charater directly.

The way I solve this issue is changing the body of format_header_param() from
```python
...
value = email.utils.encode_rfc2231(value, 'utf-8') 
value = '%s*=%s' % (name, value)
...
```
to
```python
value = '%s=%s' % (name, value)
```
Obviously, it is not a perfect way to tackle the problem since it may affect other process to use library **requests**

issue_title: Checking if message has no mentions
issue_body:
I'm writing a simple greeting command that takes a member mention as an imput.

    from discord.ext import commands

    bot = commands.Bot(command_prefix='.', description=description)

    @bot.command()
    async def greet(user: discord.User):
        await bot.say('Hello {}!'.format(user.mention))

Upon writing `.greet @user` in the chat it correctly greets a mentioned user.

I want to extend the functionality so that when someone writes `.greet` without any mentions it will send a generic response.

    @bot.command(pass_context=True)
    async def greet(ctx, user: discord.User):
        if not ctx.message.mentions:
            await bot.say('Hello everyone!')
        await bot.say('Hello {}!'.format(user.mention))

But once `.greet` is written, nothing happens. Same thing when I replace `if not` with `if len(ctx.message.mentions) == 0` or `if not user`

If I remove `not`:

    @bot.command(pass_context=True)
    async def greet(ctx, user: discord.User):
        if ctx.message.mentions:
            await bot.say('Hello everyone!')
        await bot.say('Hello {}!'.format(user.mention))

and write `.hello @user`, both messages are sent as expected.

How can I check for lack of mentions in the passed command?

issue_title: Add option to write cert to file
issue_body:
acme-tiny should offer an option (-o or --out) to write the obtained certificate to a file, instead of the standard output. 

For reference, the current documented usage is as follows:
`python acme_tiny.py --account-key ./account.key --csr ./domain.csr --acme-dir /usr/share/nginx/html/.well-known/acme-challenge/ > signed.crt`

The current workflow (writing to stdout and letting the user redirect to a file) is problematic for automated generations of certificates. 
When using the example command, signed.crt is (over)written even when the generation as failed. 
If the cert generation fails while trying to update a existing cert, the existing cert will be overwritten and lost. 

I realize the user could workaround this (redirection to temp file, checkout the file is a valid cert,  then replacing the old cert), but it would be much easier to just support the option in acme-tiny

issue_title: Should we proactively raise an error if gevent is monkeypatching threading?
issue_body:
We use `gevent` at my day job, and I hit a tricky issue where gevent was starving the `KafkaConsumer` threads, so they were timing out of the consumer groups. (There's more to it, but that's the gist.)

As a result, I am adding the following to my internal wrapper code:

    try:
        from gevent.monkey import is_module_patched
        if is_module_patched('thread'):
            raise RuntimeError("""gevent is reporting that the thread/threading
                modules are patched. This can lead to starvation of the
                kafka-python background heartbeat thread, ultimately resulting in
                the consumer being evicted from the kafka consumer group. To
                prevent this, explicitly skip monkey patching thread:
                    from gevent import monkey
                    monkey.patch_all(thread=False)
                """)
    except ImportError:  # they aren't using gevent
        continue

Wondering if we should add this directly to kafka-python as well?

We could lower it to a warning rather than a full-blown exception, in case someone has a valid reason for doing this.

This would probably be useful on `KafkaProducer` in addition to `KafkaConsumer` to minimize the chances of unexpected behavior (and subtle bugs being reported on the issue tracker).

issue_title: POST retry
issue_body:
If we POST a form (using .call or .fire as appropriate), and that POST fails due to a temporary error (e.g. local system resource / network issue), then we have lost the page content and have no way to repeat the POST. Is there a way around this? 

In one of my Ghost.py scripts, there is a chain of POST requests. If the final POST times out and leaves me with a blank browser page, then I have to reverse many steps in order to have a second attempt as the failed POST. It would be great if we could somehow take a snapshot of the current status (page content / cookies etc) and be able to roll back to it.

issue_title: Serious issue with subtrees!
issue_body:
Hi guys,

I was trying your library out, it looks great.
Yet I believe there is a design issue with how you implemented the subtrees.  Right now a tree is defined by a dictionary of nodes and the id of the root node, the hierarchy being expressed in the bpointer and fpointer of the individual node instances.

What happens when I create a subtree?  A new tree object is created, that has a subset of the nodes of the original tree.  As you mention the copy is shallow, so the nodes themselves are not deep copied. This is useful, because in principle it allows to operate on the subtrees influencing the structure of the main tree, and vice-versa.

But what if I first create a subtree out of a tree, then add a node either to this subtree or to the original tree?  What happens is that the fpointers and bpointers of the nodes in question will be updated to include the new node, but *just the nodes dictionary of either the tree or subtree will be updated*.

So when for example the function _print_backend(...) gets invoked on the tree if I modified the subtree, or on the subtree if I modified the tree, it will throw an error at the line:

```python
queue = [self[i] for i in self[nid].fpointer if filter(self[i])]
 ```
because there will be identifiers that are in the list of fpointers but NOT in the nodes of the tree/subtree.  

How would you go about fixing that?

Have a great day and thanks,

Francesco

issue_title: The dictionary AttrDict is not deepcopiable
issue_body:
The deepcopy of the AttrDict is failing as the "__getattr__" doesn't raise AttributeError for non existing attributes. 

The getattr(x, "__deepcopy__", None) is invoked internally on deepcopy.
The attribute "__deepcopy__" is not present and hence KeyError is raised by "__getitem__" but AttributeError is expected from "__getattr__"

        self.sellOrder = None

    def next(self):
        if self.buyOrder and self.sellOrder:
            return

        self.buy(exectype=bt.Order.Limit,
                 size=self.p.stake,
                 price=self.data.close[0] * 0.99,
                 valid=bt.Order.DAY)

        self.log('BUY CREATED , %.2f' % (self.data.close[0] * 0.99))

        self.sell(exectype=bt.Order.Limit,
                  size=self.p.stake,
                  price=self.data.close[0] * 1.01,
                  valid=bt.Order.DAY)

        self.log('SELL CREATED , %.2f' % (self.data.close[0] * 1.01))

    def notify_order(self, order):
        if order.status in [order.Submitted, order.Accepted]:
            if order.isbuy():
                self.buyOrder = order
            else:
                self.sellOrder = order
            return  # Await further notifications

        if order.status in [order.Completed]:
            if order.isbuy():
                self.buyOrder = None
                buytxt = 'BUY EXECUTED, %.2f' % order.executed.price
                self.log(buytxt, order.executed.dt)
            else:
                self.sellOrder = None
                selltxt = 'SELL EXECUTED, %.2f' % order.executed.price
                self.log(selltxt, order.executed.dt)
        elif order.status in [order.Expired]:
            if order.isbuy():
                self.buyOrder = None
                self.log('%s , BUY ORDER' % order.Status[order.status])
            else:
                self.sellOrder = None
                self.log('%s , SELL ORDER' % order.Status[order.status])
            if self.position:
                self.log('CLOSE POSITION , %.2f' % self.data.close[0])
                self.close(exectype=bt.Order.Close)
        elif order.status in [order.Canceled, order.Margin]:
            if order.isbuy():
                self.log('%s , BUY ORDER' % order.Status[order.status])
                self.buyOrder = None
            else:
                self.log('%s , SELL ORDER' % order.Status[order.status])
                self.sellOrder = None
            pass

    def notify_trade(self, trade):
        if trade.isclosed:
            self.log('TRADE PROFIT, GROSS %.2f, NET %.2f' %
                     (trade.pnl, trade.pnlcomm))

        elif trade.justopened:
            self.log('TRADE OPENED, SIZE %2d' % trade.size)

issue_title: optionally block propagation of keyboard events
issue_body:
Hi,

I really like your module.  It is very simple to use, and I'm using it to teach Python to middle-schoolers.

Currently, I am using this code as a demonstration (modified only slightly from your docs):

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
from pynput import keyboard
def on_press(key):
    try:
        print('Pressed: {}'.format(key.char))
    except AttributeError:
        print('Special key: {}'.format(key))

def on_release(key):
    print('Release: {}'.format(key))
    if key == keyboard.Key.esc:
        return False

with keyboard.Listener(on_press=on_press, on_release=on_release) as listener:
    while True:
        print(".")
        time.sleep(1.0)
```

It produces output like:

```
$ python test.py 
.
.
aPressed: a
Release: u'a'
.
jPressed: j
.
Release: u'j'
.
```

The problem is that each key event is propagated to the terminal, so it appears in addition to the messages.

When the program finishes, all of the output is again echoed to the bash prompt, like so:

```$ ajadajjjajddjaajajajjajajajjajajajajj```

Can you provide an optional flag or method that blocks propagation of keyboard events after they are handled by pynput?  I want to use this for a simple game, and I want the game to respond to the keyboard as a controller.  I do not want to see the keystrokes echoed to the terminal during the game or after the game.

Thanks!

issue_title: Add explainer to docs about installation issues
issue_labels: [{'id': 74802137, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/AndrewAnnex/SpiceyPy/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': True}, {'id': 717147548, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/AndrewAnnex/SpiceyPy/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': 'aee283', 'default': True}, {'id': 628277094, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/AndrewAnnex/SpiceyPy/labels/installation', 'name': 'installation', 'color': 'fe02c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 628704407, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/AndrewAnnex/SpiceyPy/labels/not%20a%20bug', 'name': 'not a bug', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
although #202 improved installation success with bare python installs, it does not totally solve the runtime dependency issue because setuptools falls back onto easy_install for the setup_requires and install_requires dependencies. This means that installations may still fail as easy_installs are a bit brittle for some transitive dependencies and ultimately inefficient for things like numpy as it does not take advantage of wheels either. Ultimately try to patch all of the contingencies will take too much effort, so it would just be better to tell users to install wheel, numpy, and urllib3[secure] in that order before trying to install on a clean python.  

see:
https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/917
https://pip.readthedocs.io/en/1.4.1/cookbook.html#controlling-setup-requires
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/

issue_title: Need to be able to downgrade Unsatisfiable to a warning
issue_body: This is blocked on Issue #11 as we need a feedback mechanism to do it, but basically Hypothesis needs to be able to run in a "no false positives" mode, which requires Unsatisfiable to not cause the tests to fail. It should instead log an error and move on.

issue_title: id3tag-coverart | not original size (substantially smaller)
issue_body: I primarily liked youtube-dl for it's parameters, but SoundScrape seems to do a fantastic job with handling SoundCloud's horrendous errors that I can't even recall the last time I didn't have one when playing any track. By default SoundScrape works the best. With options like: auto-tag (depending how the track is titled on-page) embedcoverart +1 for not having to also download the image file but instead embeds it during the downloading process. So all you have is just the track. +1 for having it work with e-mail notification links as well. Everybody is happy now, except.... PROBLEM - The coverart that's embeded to the downloaded track isn't the same size as the original trackart. NOTE - again, SoundScrape doesn't offer any parameters and works best by default so isn't there a way to control this?

issue_title: use TLS for the rendezvous connection
issue_body:
It'd be a good idea to use TLS for the client's connection to the Rendezvous server. It doesn't actually protect that much, which is why it hasn't been a priority for me before:

* an eavesdropper watching the client's connection can still tell that they're using magic-wormhole, because the packets are aimed at the (public) rendezvous server's IP address
* an eavesdropper watching the server can correlate the traffic in and out to determine that client at IP-1 is talking to the client at IP-2, even if the messages themselves are encrypted
* an eavesdropper who can listen to two likely communication partners at the same time will be able to tell that they're talking wormhole to each other, based on the timing correlation, without even looking at the server

You need Tor to protect against the first one, and even then I suspect the message pattern is distinctive enough that an eavesdropper could figure it out.

To protect against the second two, in addition to TLS (to hide the exact channel/nameplate in use) and plenty of cover traffic, we also need clients to not be online at the same time (doing something like Pond's randomly-timed server connections, and fixed-size messages). This will require a feature I'm working on called "journaled mode" to work at all.

But adding TLS is still the right thing to do. On the client side, this is pretty simple: when we parse the rendezvous server URL, make sure we can accept `wss:` in addition to just `ws:`, build a `tcp:` or `tls:` endpoint descriptor string to match, then pass that string into `endpoints.clientFromString` instead of always using `HostnameEndpoint`. (we do need to enhance the TorManager to handle this too, running TLS over Tor over TCP, which might require changes to txtorcon, I'm not sure).

On the server side, wormhole-server needs to use txsni or an `endpoints.SSL4ServerEndpoint` instead of always using `TCP4ServerEndpoint`.

To get a valid certificate for the public rendezvous server, I plan to use LetsEncrypt. I had originally thought this would require changes to wormhole-server (to let it serve a static HTML directory, to publish the ACME protocol challenge/response messages). But someone pointed out to me that TLS certificates are not bound to any specific port number (the theory being that if you can control port 80, you probably own the entire box anyways).

So I just need to run a normal webserver on the rendezvous server host, listening on port 80, and configure it to participate in the LetsEncrypt protocol for the public rendezvous URL domain (relay.magic-wormhole.io). That instance of certbot needs to write the certificate and private key into a place where wormhole-server can see it. Then wormhole-server (running on port 4000) can use the certificate without actually knowing anything about LetsEncrypt.

The order of operations needs to be:

* implement the webserver for certbot, get it running and making certificates
* implement TLS support in wormhole-server, make it possible to listen on both TLS and plain TCP at the same time (on different ports, 4000 and maybe 4443?)
* deploy it to relay.magic-wormhole.io
* implement client support
* commit a change to cli/public_relay.py, changing the URL to use `wss:` and point at the new port
* make a release

If I use just `\r\n\. ` as the regex, if I run `di ". text"`, pexpect gets confused because it's looking for the next linesep + dot + space as the prompt, but the first one it finds is the result, and not the next line prompt. I believe all prompts have a full empty line before them, so I made `\r\n\r\n\. ` the regex to find the dot prompt, but this failed on Linux on my machine whenever it did a shell command. `!ls` would have the \x1b ANSI escape code returned from the shell, and wouldn't match the double linesep. The lookbehind instead of just matching them leaves an extra newline between results. Otherwise the next row of [2]: would be right up against [1]:

No, `exit` doesn't work. This is because pexpect is waiting for a response from the spawned Stata console, but that has already closed. I need to figure out how to fix this. See https://github.com/kylebarron/stata_kernel/issues/7

I plan to refactor the code a good amount. I'd say the code is relatively clean for having written it quickly, but I want to step back a minute and try to think through an optimal structure for the package. I'm thinking of moving all the code that deals with validating input into a code_manager.py file (see https://github.com/kylebarron/stata_kernel/issues/32 for initial thoughts). Hopefully that will parse input and not send code to Stata unless it's sure that it can be run safely (once I turn off the timeout option, things will run forever if invalid input is sent to the console). Ideally this would also use tokens for autocompletions, though I'll probably put that into a separate file.

Then a separate run.py file for sending the validated code to Stata and retrieving output. I'm happy to have devised a function that allows me to abstract the differences between sending code with Windows Automation vs Mac Applescript. But there's still differences on each platform with retrieving output. There are some issues with retrieving graphs, especially, and apparently Stata 14 doesn't export SVG?

issue_body:
I'm extracting contacts from an EWS account (Office 365 hosted):

```
exchangelib.protocol.BaseProtocol.SESSION_POOLSIZE = 1
…
account = exchangelib.Account(…)
query = account.contacts.all()
contacts = list(item for item in query.iterator() if isinstance(item, exchangelib.items.Contact))
```

The account in question has ~267 contacts. This usually works well, but it sometimes gets stuck forever in `Waiting for GetItem._get_elements result n of n` ([services.py:453](https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/master/exchangelib/services.py#L453)), as if `r.get()` never returned.

Here's part of the log (removing the full request headers / responses, I can provide the entire log via email): https://gist.github.com/adrinavarro/82672f4c6c7612ba4e187aef977400a0

I've seen this happen as well before "SESSION_POOLSIZE" was adjusted (this is because we hit Office365 for several accounts at once and at some point it felt a bit like a DDos).

issue_title: Add more images to recognize
issue_body:
* face_recognition version:
* Python version:
* Operating System:

### Description

Hi there sir I just want to say thank you for the face recognition tutorial and codes. I just want to ask how can I add at least 2 more person to recognize. Below is the sample code i already edited. Thanks for the reply nice project again sir...

### What I Did

```
# I tried to add another image then change some code(s) but unfortunately it didn't come up as planned hehehe... :D
# This is a demo of running face recognition on a Raspberry Pi.
# This program will print out the names of anyone it recognizes to the console.

# To run this, you need a Raspberry Pi 2 (or greater) with face_recognition and
# the picamera[array] module installed.
# You can follow this installation instructions to get your RPi set up:
# https://gist.github.com/ageitgey/1ac8dbe8572f3f533df6269dab35df65

import face_recognition
import picamera
import numpy as np

# Get a reference to the Raspberry Pi camera.
# If this fails, make sure you have a camera connected to the RPi and that you
# enabled your camera in raspi-config and rebooted first.
camera = picamera.PiCamera()
camera.resolution = (320, 240)
output = np.empty((240, 320, 3), dtype=np.uint8)

# Load a sample picture and learn how to recognize it.
print("Loading known face image(s)")
roei_image = face_recognition.load_image_file("roei.jpg")
roei_face_encoding = face_recognition.face_encodings(roei_image)[0]
iris_image = face_recognition.load_image_file("iris.jpg")
iris_face_encoding = face_recognition.face_encodings(iris_image)[1]

# Initialize some variables
face_locations = []
face_encodings = []

while True:
    print("Capturing image.")
    # Grab a single frame of video from the RPi camera as a numpy array
    camera.capture(output, format="rgb")

    # Find all the faces and face encodings in the current frame of video
    face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(output)
    print("Found {} faces in image.".format(len(face_locations)))
    face_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(output, face_locations)

    # Loop over each face found in the frame to see if it's someone we know.
    for face_encoding in face_encodings:
        # See if the face is a match for the known face(s)
        match = face_recognition.compare_faces([iris_face_encoding], face_encoding)
        match = face_recognition.compare_faces([roei_face_encoding], face_encoding)
        name = "<Unknown Person>"

        if match[0]:
            name = "Roei Boribor"
        elif match[1]:
            name = "Iris Javier"

        print("I see someone named {}!".format(name))

```

issue_body:
* face_recognition version: face-recognition (1.0.0), face-recognition-models (0.3.0)
* Python version: 3.5.2
* Operating System: Ubuntu-16.04-LTS x86_64

### Description
First of all, thank you for your excellent work!

I'm just confused about this:
What does the method `face_recognition.load_image_file(path)` do when loading a file? I'm asking this because I found that this method returns a slightly different result compared to `cv2.imread(path)` when loading the same `.jpg` image. This means, if I do these:
```
In [1]: import cv2, face_recognition, numpy as np

In [2]: img_fr = face_recognition.load_image_file('/home/miles/三十.jpg')[:,:,::-1]
          # use [:,:,::-1] to convert RGB -> BGR
          # "三十.jpg" is a photo containing exactly containing one face.

In [3]: img_cv = cv2.imread('/home/miles/三十.jpg')

In [4]: img_fr.shape
Out[4]: (424, 301, 3)

In [5]: img_cv.shape
Out[5]: (424, 301, 3)

In [6]: difcount = 424*301*3 - np.sum(img_fr==img_cv)

In [7]: difcount
Out[7]: 56072
```
There will be 56072 different pixels. Moreover, if I compute the distance:
```
In [1]: import cv2, face_recognition, numpy as np

In [2]: img_fr = face_recognition.load_image_file('/home/miles/三十.jpg')[:,:,::-1]

In [3]: img_cv = cv2.imread('/home/miles/三十.jpg')

In [4]: location_fr = face_recognition.face_locations(img_fr)

In [5]: location_cv = face_recognition.face_locations(img_cv)

In [6]: encodings_fr = face_recognition.face_encodings(img_fr, location_fr)

In [7]: encodings_cv = face_recognition.face_encodings(img_cv, location_cv)

In [8]: dis = face_recognition.face_distance(encodings_fr, encodings_cv[0])

In [9]: dis
Out[9]: array([ 0.03327288])
```
It won't be ZERO while it should be, because they are actually the same image. This took me 2 hours to find out as I was implementing a bit-of-complicated app. I'm curious about how the difference comes. Is it caused by floating number error when decoding the `.jpg` file?

issue_title: Constraints
issue_body:
Currently, the package does not have a way to implement constraints into its optimization process.  For example, one cannot [exclude points](https://github.com/fmfn/BayesianOptimization/issues/39).  The linked discussion suggests that generalizing the exclusion problem to formal constraints is the better way to go.
 
After some investigation, I've found that the implementation will be pretty easy.  Since the ``acq_max`` function uses SciPy's ``minimize`` function, it's simply a matter of allowing the user to optionally include constraints in the instantiation method and passing those constraints into ``minimize`` through ``acq_max``.  We'll also have to switch the optimization method from L-BFGS-B to SLSQP when the user passes constraints.
 
The only question I have about the switch is whether to give the user an option to pass the Jacobian of constraint functions.  ``minimize`` has an option for that, but it only speeds the optimization up in [some cases](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35826828/should-slsqp-minimization-speed-up-with-an-analytic-jacobian).  Perhaps it's unnecessary.
 
I'll do this ASAP.

This can be done easily by attaching a post_save signal to LogEntry model:
```python
from django.contrib.admin.models import LogEntry
from django.db.models.signals import post_save
from django.dispatch import receiver

@receiver(post_save, sender=LogEntry, dispatch_uid="clear_fancy_cache")
def clear_fancy_cache(sender, instance, **kwargs):
     #clear cache
```

issue_title: core.loading fails to load modules new style Django Apps
issue_body:
Django since 1.7 now [supports](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.7/ref/applications/) listing the application's config class in `INSTALLED_APPS` instead of module name.

This breaks [core.loading.load](https://github.com/ellmetha/django-precise-bbcode/blob/develop/precise_bbcode/core/loading.py), as it expect everything to be module names.

issue_title: polling and websocket transports do not close connections
issue_body:
Every `SocketIO` session causes two connections to be created, one for the initial `XHR_PollingTransport` and one for the `WebsocketTransport`.

When the upgrade from polling to websocket happens, the TCP connection for the polling transport is not closed, resulting in a resource leak.

Additionally, calling `disconnect()` on the `SocketIO` instance leaves the websocket transport's connection in a `CLOSE_WAIT` state.

issue_title: transport error after background task emit
issue_body:
First, it happened with flask-socketio, but then I used only python-socketio and got the same error. The scenario is the following:

* A docker container with Flask + flask-/python-socketio that only emits (no listening);
* Flask also send tasks to celery;
* A second docker container has a redis/rabbitmq (tried both) server used by both socketio and Celery;
* A third container has a Celery worker that emits the results line by line.

Steps to reproduce:

1. Start the Flask container
2. POST a task so Flask send it to Celery
3. Start a worker container

Then, the browser disconnects with "transport error" that is not caught in the server (socketio.on_default_error) and I have no more info about the error. After reconnection, it seems to never disconnect again, working as expected. This error seems to happen once after a node is added/removed and there is an emit from the worker.

After the 3 steps, I added another task so we can see that the 2nd task results are fine. The below is from the web page (strings like `c68c7d611e22` are hostnames):
```
WebSocket is connected.
08:04:14 Web server c68c7d611e22 received Task 1
08:04:23 Worker 45715df9950e is up.
08:04:24 Worker 45715df9950e started  Task 1
Reconnecting (transport error)... WebSocket is connected.
08:12:30 Web server c68c7d611e22 received Task 2
08:12:30 Worker 45715df9950e started  Task 2
08:12:34 Worker 45715df9950e finished Task 2
```
If I don't use containers for Flask and worker, it seems to work or it's not that reproducible.

issue_title: Avoid weak RNG unless user explicitly permits it
issue_body:
I think it would be a good idea to avoid falling back to weak RNG (`random.Random`) unless the user has explicitly expressed that they are OK with it. This is on the idea that it is often better for the command to fail than to (unknowingly) get a weakly random result. 

In case of normal evocation, lack of secure random (`random.SystemRandom`) would cause the command to fail. The error message can instruct the user to try again with e.g. `--allow-weak-rng` option if they are OK with weak random.

I am willing to implement this if you think it is a good idea.

issue_title: Root API url without trailing slash location is missing in resource URL
issue_body:
### How to reproduce
1. Initialize API url with root URL with a location segment, but without trailing slash, i.e.:
```python
API(api_root_url='https://test.loc/api')
```
2. Add a resource.
3. Invoke any method of the resource.

### Expected result
I expect one of 2 options:
1. A resource URL uses root URL with trailing slash (i.e. `https://test.loc/api/resource`).
2. An exception is raised pointing to wrong configuration.

I like the first one a lot better. If there are any objections, please shout.

### Actual result
A resource URL without root URL path is used, i.e. `https://test.loc/resource`.

### Root cause
[This](https://github.com/allisson/python-simple-rest-client/blob/ffb53d0f42630b79ad544a5b53578b0389dcb120/simple_rest_client/resource.py#L75) line.

issue_title: document.update_field() raises 409 after successful retry
issue_body:
## Bug Description

### 1. Steps to reproduce and the simplest code sample possible to demonstrate the issue

The `Document.update_field()` method raises an HTTPError(409) exception if it succeeds with its retries without exhausting the max retries.

Here is the code in `Document._update_field()` (see [here](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/master/src/cloudant/document.py#L262)):

```
        # Attempt to save, retrying conflicts up to max_tries.
        try:
            self.save()
        except requests.HTTPError as ex:
            if tries < max_tries and ex.response.status_code == 409:
                self._update_field(
                    action, field, value, max_tries, tries=tries+1)
            raise
```

The `raise` statement should be in an `else:` clause if the previous `if` statement.

If you need a script that reproduces this, let me know, but it is too long to paste here (I think), and from looking at the code, the issue should be obvious.

### 2. What you expected to happen

The `Document.update_field()` method should not raise an HTTPError(409) exception if it ended up with a successful retry without exhausting its max retries. Only if the max retries are exhausted, should it raise the exception.

The description of the `Document.update_field()` method should document that.

There should be test cases that verify that case. :-)

### 3. What actually happened

See above.

## Environment details

- Version(s) that are affected by this issue.
    > 2.9.0
- Python version
    > 2.7.14

issue_title: How to dynamically bind executor with variable symbol?
issue_body:
During testing phase, my symbol's computing structure depends on some factor K. E.g, when the factor equals to K, then there are N(K) layers of Convolution operations, where N(K) changes with K.
Thus, I have to construct a new symbol and rebind it with an executor when K changes. Code may look like this:
```
if current_k != last_k:
    sym = get_symbol(current_k)
    mod = mx.mod.Module(sym, ...)
    mod.bind(...)
else:
    # keep the module unchanged
    pass
```
My question is, how to effectively release memory allocated by the bound module and rebind it with a different symbol?
I've tried using python's 'del' to delete the bound module, but the memory is not released when look into the info returned by 'nvidia-smi'.

issue_title: DeepSpeech exporting INT64 instead of expected INT32 into SparseToDence opp, not friendly to mobile TensorFlow.
issue_body:
tf.sparse_to_dense should be casted to be only INT32 parameters instead of INT64 default
https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/d68fde8d7af91cd0a2519f283fe559576e0f43da/DeepSpeech.py#L1697

I used Tensorflow 1.6 on Ubuntu 16.04

The lack of casting makes the model import crash on iOS and android, and requires to post processing  to be mobile friendly.
[tf.sparse_tensor_to_dense(sparse_tensor) for sparse_tensor in decoded], name='output_node')

should be 

[tf.sparse_tensor_to_dense(tf.to_int32(sparse_tensor)) for sparse_tensor in decoded], name='output_node')

There are probably better solution that a brutal C like casting.

issue_title: List of Enum values fails
issue_body:
Hi

This might be a duplicate of issue #140 , if so I apologies, but this is a simpler test case.

It looks like something is going wrong but I can't pin down whether it is the encoder or decoder. I have spent a few hours trying to trace this through but I have not come up with a fix.

When the JSON is decoded, the second element of the list should be a reference to the C.VALUE object, but instead it is a raw list [1,2,3].

Here is a minimal test case.

This is a simple workaround, which is to use a tuple for the VALUE list rather than a list but I thought it worth reporting the issue.

```
from enum import Enum
from jsonpickle import encode, decode


class C(Enum):
    VALUE = [1, 2, 3]


my_list = [C.VALUE, C.VALUE]

assert decode(encode(my_list))[1] == my_list[1]
```

Best regards and thank you for jsonpickle - it is fantastically useful.

Richard

issue_title: Struct packing issue...
issue_body:
Hi,

I was having all sorts of problems loading 3DS files and finally narrowed it down to sizeof( Discreet3DS::Chunk ) returning 8 when it should have been returning 6, ie: the PACK_STRUCT macro wasn't working.

A bit more research and it looks like using __attribute__ to control struct packing in mingw (which is what I'm using) is broken and has been for a long time! I'm using mingw-64 6.2.0 which is relatively recent and there are reports of this going back to 2012.

But it turns out that mingw supports #pragma pack too, slightly modified: #pragma pack(1) to enable packing, #pragma pack() to disable so I changed pushpack1.h and poppack1.h to use this instead and 3DS is now working! I recommend you guys change this in the repos too.

Bye,
Mark

issue_title: stop using multiprocessing
issue_body:
Several interesting issues have arisen in my many hours fighting multiprocessing. The most obvious is the issue with forwarding stdin to the subprocess which requires terrible hacks, especially on windows.

However another much more subtle issue is this... on unix multiprocessing by default does not fork+exec. It only forks. This is interesting because it uses the original python interpreter. The issue is that any libraries that were already imported are not re-imported into the child process because they are already found in memory. Instead we would prefer to fork+exec a new subprocess which uses its own interpreter to get a fresh system. To be more clear, if you import a library prior to `hupper.start_reloader` and then you try to develop on that library, it will not be reloaded even though hupper says it restarted.

issue_title: Custom field for Billing agreements.
issue_body:
In the classic API you could set a custom field which would be echoed back to your listener, this was helpful because then you could use this field to find what payment was for which user and so on.

However in the rest API there is no such field for Billing agreements however there is for single payments, so I was wondering would this be added?

For anyone that is having issues with this as well I have found a work around and that is on creation of the billing agreement regex the execute token from the approval_url link and then store this token along with the associated user who is subscribing and then on execution you can look up the token and find the  user again.

More insight on this issue  would be awesome, thanks.

```sh
~/path/of/work/dir > pipenv shell
Loading .env environment variables…
Spawning environment shell (/usr/bin/fish). Use 'exit' to leave.

issue_title: warning about MANIFEST exclude rule refers to "included files"
issue_body:
In a file `MANIFEST.in` containing a rule of the form `exclude foo`, running `python setup.py install` produces:

```
warning: no previously-included files found matching 'foo'
```

which I believe originates from:

https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/7d56dc12c196dc48fe30918fea7a6aac90182c89/setuptools/command/egg_info.py#L335-L337

It seems that "included" refers to the excluded file(s). This observation may be relevant also to other lines in the file `egg_info.py`.

issue_title: Producer can't see a disconnected server
Using Streamparse in HDP 2.3.2 - Storm version does not get parsed
issue_body:
Hi,

I've been trying to set up Streamparse 3.4.0 within HDP 2.3.2, using Python 2.7.8. I was having issues running `submit`, for which it gave me an error in the `local_storm_version()` function within the `streamparse/util.py` file. Upon testing, I found that the list returned by the Regex pattern find is empty.

```python
pattern = r'^Storm ([0-9.]+)'
return parse_version(re.findall(pattern, res.stdout, flags=re.MULTILINE)[0])
```

The string returned by the "storm version" command on HDP 2.3.2 is:

    Running: /usr/lib/jvm/java-1.7.0-openjdk-1.7.0.91.x86_64/bin/java -client ..(omitted text).. supervisor/conf backtype.storm.utils.VersionInfo
    Storm 0.10.0.2.3.2.0-2950
    URL git@github.com:hortonworks/storm.git -r f417df29609d4477b485b4cb01c6e496f0715bab
    Branch (HEAD detached at f417df2)
    Compiled by jenkins on 2015-09-30T20:17Z
    From source with checksum 4a2753c9efa4bb8463ea768bf1561412

The important part is in bold. The funny thing is that between the huge line before it and the actual line with the version, there is no newline character. And on top of that, HDP Storm uses hyphens in its version numbers. These two issues cause the Python code to not correctly parse the version number. The fix I was able to come up with to solve this is

```python
pattern = r'Storm ([0-9.-]+)'
```

But I have not tested this for other Storm deployments. I need to automate the setup of Streamparse for my application, so I would like this fix to be in the actual Streamparse sources. Is it worth submitting a pull-request or is there another way I can get around this?

Thanks!

issue_title: Modeling-related changes to PSF/PRF photometry
issue_body:
Comments from @eteq, copied from #26:

* First, a few comments about how `astropy.modeling` is used here.  It looks to me like `DiscretePRF` and `GaussianPSF` are the *only* allowed classes, rather than arbitrary models from `astropy.modeling.models`.  This seems to mostly stem from the use of the `fit` method of the PSF/PRF classes.  Why is this necessary?  I can certainly imagine wanting to try other PSF models (most crucially, the Moffat function).  So I think it would be better if `DiscretePRF` and `GaussianPSF` obeyed standard convnetions for `Fittable2DModel`s, and the work of massaging those models into a form appropriate for `psf_photometry` should be done elsewhere (either a container class or in the `psf_photometry` function itself.  Otherwise, we effectively have two slightly different model-fitting frameworks 
* Why doesn't `GaussianPSF` use `modeling.models.Gaussian2D`? It seems to have implemented all the gaussian behavior separately?
* Why is the `fitter` hard-coded into the PSF/PRF class instead of following the convention used in `astro.modeling` of having it be a separate object?  Don't we want to allow other fitting methods?
* I think it would make sense to try to make `psf_photometry` and `aperture_photometry` more consistent.  E.g., right now `aperture_photometry` gets its centers from 2 arguments `xc` and `yx`, while `psf_photometry` accepts a single `positions` argument.  At the very least, these should be the same. It also might be useful to allow using `aperture_photometry` apertures to define the region in which a star is fit (although maybe that's more of a feature request).

issue_title: Should kwarg unit override header BUNIT
issue_body:
I got a bug report saying that ``aperture_photometry`` fails as the current code requires the ``unit`` kwarg to be the same unit as the header ``BUNIT``. However ``BUNIT`` is ``'JY/BEAM'`` in this case which is unrecognisable by the parser, and thus ``unit`` cannot override it with the correct format.

I cannot find any notes why we chose to have this requirement and not the other way, but propose the following two options to solve this:
  1) Have ``unit`` override ``BUNIT`` (and maybe other ``dataunit``, too)
  2) Check if ``BUNIT`` is Unrecognizable, and if yes allow ``unit`` to override.

I prefer the second option, but if your experience says that ``BUNIT`` tend to be wrong in some cases, then I see the logic doing the first option. PR to follow when this is decided.

cc: @astrofrog, @larrybradley

issue_title: Don't have src directory
issue_body: For pytest plugins, it's not common to put the source inside the ``src`` directory. So the ``pytest_remotedata`` directory should be moved up to the top level.

issue_title: WebSearch: hide "deprecated" (or/and restricted) collections in "Search collections" list
issue_body:
Originally on 2013-06-17

The search interface displays a list of all available collections under the "Search collections" label. This enables users to restrict queries to some selected collections. The list of collections is built using all available collections, including those not attached to the main collections tree. This might be an issue if some collections have been removed from the tree (replaced for example by other) but left unattached to keep links to it. It would therefore be nice if some checkbox on each collection settings would allow to remove it from this list.

Alternatively one could consider that only collection attached to the tree would be included in the list.

Additionally one could decide to filter restricted collections from this list.

issue_title: RFC git signatures (kill, simplify or status quo)
issue_body:
# Problem

Related to RFC #2856.

Currently we have the following commit message directives (see [here](http://invenio.readthedocs.org/en/latest/developers/git-workflow.html#r2b-commit-message-qa-review-directives)):

```
Signed-off-by
Reported-by
Co-authored-by
Improved-by
Acked-by
Tested-by
Reviewed-by
```

You can broadly categorise them in two types:
* directives related to giving credit (co-authored-by, reported-by)
* directives related to quality stamping (reviewed-by, signed-off-by)

For new contributors it's not easy to know which to use when, and additionally it's not clear if the differences in signatures is actually used.

# Proposal

Simplify the possible commit directives to:

* ``Signed-off-by`` - (Related to quality) Used by authors, testers and reviewers to sign that a given commit adheres to Invenio's quality standards and to the best of their knowledge is ready to be merged. 
* ``Co-authored-by`` - (Related to credit) Used to acknowledge significant co-authorship including when a person significantly improves the code after the original author left.

and exceptionally:

* ``Reported-by`` - Use only in exceptional circumstances, when there's no GitHub issue (could happen when an external user reports a bug report via email, and not issue was created).

Note: Tester/reviewers should only sign commits they can fully confirm the quality off. Blanco signing of commits may negatively impact their merit and credibility. For commits to be **considered** for integration a minimum of two ``Signed-off-by`` signatures are needed. That a commit is considered for integration, does not mean the commit actually will be integrated.

issue_title: RFC: bibupload has no activated tests
issue_body:
#### Pretext
Some branches were hit by a `bibupload` bug not long ago and this has cost us some development time. I believe the patch that fixed it was 4e4225b7f1dcd7688c3d42b5be8675ceed6f8428.

#### Problem
From this it seems that there is nothing checking if basic `bibupload` uploading is working in travis.

#### Current state
Integration tests for `bibupload` exist in `invenio-demosite`, but the demosite has never been tested in `.travis.yml` from what its git log reveals.

#### Solution 1
We can make the `demosite` part of the `invenio`'s travis test run. To do this cleanly, we would have to run the tests as they do currently, and __then__ test again with the demosite. After all, we should be able to, at a minimum, run the demosite record creation.

#### Solution 2
If including the `demosite` in the travis of `invenio` is deemed too much, we can add a couple of [simple black-box integration tests](https://github.com/dset0x/invenio/commit/5cb7ac6930672887ab0c1a4fcd7137df574f9ec9), so that we will at least not have developers who want to add some sample records in their database for testing purposes spend hours trying to figure out what's broken.

issue_title: spires: problem with author search
issue_body:
Originally by hoc on 2012-05-15 as inveniosoftware/invenio#1062

There's a problem with searching via initials versus full-names.

find a pi, s y not a Pi, So Young
in SPIRES this has (correctly) a zero result, because all
Pi, So Young should be indexed to Pi, S Y
so this search would only find a non-zero result if there were, say,
Pi, Shan Yu (and there isn't, all S.Y. Pi are So Young Pi).

Looking at something where dashes aren't an issue:
find a crewther, r j not a crewther, rodney james
should find zero but instead finds all the papers not listing his full name.

or "C. Quigg" 
find a quigg, c not a quigg, curtis
gets 113 of his 248 papers, it just weeds out the ones he's signed as "Chris Quigg"

issue_title: Run ZODB cache garbage collection when returning long lazy result sets
issue_body:
Background: if `ZCatalog.threshold` is set to non-None value then `ZCatalog.catalog_objects()` calls  `Connection.cacheGC()` after eash `threshold` calls. That helps client code to limit its memory usage during large reindexing operations.

Reading large result sets from `Catalog.search()` can produce problem similar to mass reindexing: storing too many objects in the ZODB cache, overcoming cache size limits and triggering MemoryOverflow.

Let's consider the use case: ZCatalog with >100K entries, a query that returns ~10K results and client code loop that calculates aggregate from values stored in one of metadata columns.

Such a loop instantiates 10K non-persistent "brains" records. Each "brain" record fills its data with the values pulled from metadata columns. Catalog metadata is stored as IOBTree. Reading one document record from IOBTree fetches the whole IOBucket object that contains metadata for up to 60 catalog entries. 100K big IOBTree has average height of two-three layers and fetching one leaf bucket from the tree bottom requires reading also several intermediate buckets.

With uniform distribution most of 10K result records would fetch metadata from different IOBuckets and reading them all will pull the whole metadata IOBTree into ZODB cache.

Proposal: call `Connection.cacheGC()` after instantiating each `ZCatalog.threshold` "brains" records. Use the same mechanism as is used for transaction savepoint during `ZCatalog.catalog_object()`, but do not make transaction savepoints, only run cache garbage collection.

issue_title: Feature request / Idea / Poll: Create kex packages
issue_body:
I encountered a new and an interesting way how to look at the android apps that would work in a similar way as the launcher is at [SO](http://stackoverflow.com/q/40238909/5994041). Or even extend the launcher itself.

I'm not really sure how to do it, but p4a would need an option to compile a package, not package it into apk, which then could be packaged into _[`kex`](https://kivy.org/docs/api-kivy.ext.html)_ file. It's directed probably the same way as the idea with already compiled builds to be able to work on Windows. Most likely it's already there, just an argument(s) missing to run recipes only.

Then there could be a bool in `App` class, which would have a method that already loads `kex` files from a location (SD card?).

It might be overkill, but it doesn't really have to be, because:

* smaller App size (interpreter only)
* more flexibility, packages could be packaged as a standalone (non-runable) applications like e.g. some libraries or hidden apps on Android i.e. plugins for an application
* more flexible package updates

Yet there's a con - if not secure enough, a user could misuse packages and well... get into the app, get the source and all other fancy stuff just by playing with a package loading. This con is not present if talking only about the use of launcher.

It's basically almost the same as placing a compiled package into a folder with `main.py`, but more user-friendly or beginner-proof. I'm not sure if this is a good way for p4a or not, so... if you can, vote here(those github post "reactions"), or just close if it's too offtopic. 😄

issue_title: Better handling of argument name typos
issue_body:
Using this minimal example of a Python-fire CLI:

```python
import fire

def f(my_var):
    print(my_var)

fire.Fire(my_var)
```

This is the result I get:

```bash
➜  ~ python firez.py --my-var=hello
hello
➜  ~ python firez.py --myvar=hello
--myvar=hello
```

As you can see, a typo in the argument leads to unexpected behaviour, which can be really hard to debug.

I would suggest never treating an argument matching `--[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+` as positional, without some sort of escaping by the user (perhaps similar to the escaping you require to avoid parsing ints, etc.).

issue_title: Read variables from either the CLI or from the environment
issue_body:
Hello,

I have been using Fire for a while now and in the last few days, it struck me that it may be nice to have a way to read a *required* variable either as an argument or as an environment variable.

The example that made me think about this was a simple script I wrote to execute some tasks in Facebook's API. It needs to receive both the _application id_ and _secret_. Sometimes I leave those as environment variables and sometimes I do not, depending on the machine that receives the deployed code.

I solved the issue by doing something like:

```python
from os import environ
from fire import Fire

APP_ID = environ('APP_ID')

def main(app_id=None):
    assert app_id or APP_ID, 'You need to provide the app ID either as a parameter or as an environment variable'

if __name__ ==  '__main__':
    main()
```

This got me wondering if it would be nice or even useful to implement something like this directly into Fire's variable handling. If so, I would love to contribute.

Thank you all very much :D

issue_title: Yet Another Organization Discussion
issue_body:
Hello all,

once more I would like to propose a couple of stuff for the organization of NeuroM in a more intuitive way. I will just write down my ideas and we can discuss it over time. I would appreciate it if we can have a fruitful discussion upon these ideas.

First of all, I think it would be easier and tidier if we had a dedicated module for features where we can develop the latter without having to modify the neuron/population ezy objects. Regardless of the design of the model I think it would be better to have the minimum possible amount of code inside the core objects --the way it is right now-- and then remove entirely the ezy module. This way it will be clear for the user what are the main structures in the software and what are they for.

The connection between that module and the neuron/population objects can vary. It can be in an object oriented way, i.e. Neuron.features which set as an attribute upon initialization:

```python
class Neuron:
    def __init__(self,...):
        self.features = NeuronFeatures(self)

segment_lengths = nrn.features.segment_lengths(*args, **kwargs)
```
 in semi-functional way (maybe it is considered object-oriented, dunno):
```python
fs = NeuronFeatures(nrn)
fs.section_lengths(*args, **kwargs)
```
which can be alternatively implemented in a more general way and create feature objects regardless of the abstraction level through a factory class:
```python
fs = Features(object_with_features)
```
or in a completely functional way:
```python
fs = features(object_with_features, Features.segment_lengths)
```
It is important to stress here that in the last block of code in the features function one will not pass the function itself i.e. segments.foo, but an Enum for the feature. This is mainly the interface for the users in order to be able to see all the available features in a consistent way and not have to search for the specific function in the low or high levels of the code to extract what they need. 

What do you think?

issue_title: coverage % is misleading
issue_body:
We're getting very good coverage percentages out of `runtests htmlcov` but the numbers are quite misleading: at least some of the unittests are not actually verifying the returned data properly: e.g. `test_experiment.test__feature_check__computation()` doesn't verify the feature check of the `feature` feature...

We should:

- [ ] push all unittests as low as possible in the function call structure (e.g. check the feature check directly, not through the class-level interface) so that it is clearer what needs to be checked
- [ ] when writing unittests, indicate clearly (if only in the function docstrings) whether some checks have not been implemented yet, or better:
- [ ] do not call functions in a unittest unless you are sure you have comprehensively checked the return

In any case when we are performing a complex operation on a data-set (like a feature-check of a dataframe) we must be careful to really check the return: otherwise all the code that gets hit by the called operation will be marked as 'covered' but will not actually have been tested!

_this is a very general problem... I'm sure others have had the same issue... I wonder how they've dealt with it?_

issue_title: Not passing get params to controller function
issue_body:
I have a swagger endpoint like this:

```
paths:
  /query:
    get:
      summary: Query material's information
      description: |
        This interface using application token to authorize a client, and return the material's information under specified orderId.
      parameters:
        - name: token
          in: query
          description: Api token of the client.
          required: true
          type: string
        - name: order_id
          in: query
          description: Target orderId.
          required: true
          type: number
          format: int64
      tags:
        - Query
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Material informations.
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/MaterialInfo'
        '401':
          description: Invalid token.
        default:
          description: Unexpected error
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/Error'
```
And I have used swagger editor to generate a server, I got two _.py_ files:
**app.py:**
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import connexion

if __name__ == '__main__':
   app = connexion.App(__name__, 8889, specification_dir='./swagger/')
   app.add_api('swagger.yaml', arguments={'title': 'Server-to-server api for querying material information of PLUS platform.'})
   app.run()
```
**default_controller.py:**
```

def query_get(token, orderId) -> str:
   return 'do some magic!'
```
It looks fine.
I have tried using some wrong params, it returns a good 400 status with error information.


But when I using correct params, here comes **500** with no details information.
I have read the documents and connexion-examples, it looks like nothing wrong with this generated code above.



And after reading the connexion's code and debugging, I found that **connexion call _query_get_ with no arguments.**
There was an "_Exception: query_get() missing 2 required positional arguments: 'token' and 'orderId' _" raised.

I changed **default_controller.py** to:
```
from flask import request


def query_get():
    token = request.args.get("token")
    orderId = request.args.get("order_id")
    return "ok"
```
Then this endpoint works fine.


In this case, connexion verified the params but forget to pass it to controller function.

(PS: I'm using python 3.4.3 with connexion 1.0.49)

issue_title: Please publish python universal wheel
issue_body:
**What are wheels?**
Wheels are the new standard of Python distribution and are intended to replace eggs. Support is offered in pip >= 1.4 and setuptools >= 0.8.

**Advantages of wheels**
Faster installation for pure Python and native C extension packages.
Avoids arbitrary code execution for installation. (Avoids setup.py)
Installation of a C extension does not require a compiler on Windows or macOS.
Allows better caching for testing and continuous integration.
Creates .pyc files as part of installation to ensure they match the Python interpreter used.
More consistent installs across platforms and machines.

https://pythonwheels.com/

Must appreciated!

issue_title: Python - Library flags ignored when preceded by custom argparse flags
issue_body:
For the following program `tmp.py`, in Python 2.7.5:

```python
from argparse import ArgumentParser
from dynet import *

def main(args):
    pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = ArgumentParser(description="minimum test case")
    parser.add_argument('--dynet-mem')
    parser.add_argument('--test')
    args = parser.parse_args()
    main(args)
```

Memory is allocated correctly by dynet in the following invocation:
```
> python /disk/scratch/nsaphra/dynamic_curriculum/tmp.py --dynet-mem 1000 --test 1
[dynet] random seed: 2748060846
[dynet] allocating memory: 1000MB
[dynet] memory allocation done.
```


However, the same flag is ignored in the following invocation:
```
> python /disk/scratch/nsaphra/dynamic_curriculum/tmp.py --test 1 --dynet-mem 1000
[dynet] random seed: 915213834
[dynet] allocating memory: 512MB
[dynet] memory allocation done.
```

issue_title: CyclicalSGDTrainer: learning_rate never updated with default Trainer constructor
issue_body:
Hi,
I don't know if this is a bug, but it really is counter-intuitive!

In the Cyclical SGD trainer contructor, gamma is set to zero by default which cancels any learning_rate update
See function cyclic_update_eta, l.249 in current master.
The update is multiplied by the itth power of gamma, which is zero by default. See:

https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/b07411ca75e74afe3f19f6fbda5646c5ddd6f4b2/dynet/training.h#L249

issue_title: Reactived Users should be able to log in
issue_body:
When a user account is inactivated by the AccountExpiryBackend, in most cases the admins will want to re-activate the users. They will do that by going into the Admin Site, set is_active to True and save the user.

However, the last_login of the User is still an old date, so when the just re-activated user tries to log in they are again inactivated by the same AccountExpiryBackend.
Users should be able to log in after an admin re-activated their account.

The same thing happens when the User fails to enter the right password LOGIN_FAILURE_LIMIT times and it is inactivated because of that.
The admin page for LoginAttemptAdmin has an Activate link that will reset the failed logins counter, but the admin is expected to know that the user will have to be re-activated using the link in that page.
It would be nicer to let the admin activate users in the normal way and reset the counter on re-activation instead.

issue_title: Turn off incrementalism on a VTS
issue_body: Related to #2531 Square has a use case for invalidating incrementalism at a finer grained level which we talked about on slack.  We have an annotation processor that has a non-java source input.  We would like to tell the java_libary VTS that it is not just invalid, but that incrementalism should be turned off for that compile.  Currently we force this with a custom task and target by finding the zinc .pants.d output dir for the target and removing the analysis file in conjunction with `--compile-zinc-clear-invalid-analysis` flag being enabled. This is less than ideal from a number of standpoints.

issue_title: Process-specific rule syntax
issue_body:
As discussed with @illicitonion (and previously on #4289), we will likely want to make some process-specific syntax for rules, since they are guaranteed to need to wrap inputs and outputs (which would otherwise just be Snapshots and Snapshots, or ExecuteProcessRequests and ExecuteProcessResults) in order to disambiguate them from other process executions.

An expanded version of the syntax that we came up with was:
```
@process(ClasspathEntry, [Select(Javac), SelectProjection(Snapshot, SourcesField, 'source_field', Target)])
def example(binary, sources_snapshot):
  out_dir = "doesnt_matter"
  process_request = SnapshottedProcessRequest(args=(binary.name,
                                                    '-d', out_dir)+
                                                    tuple(f.path for f in sources_snapshot.files),
                                              snapshots=(binary.snapshot, sources_snapshot),
                                              directories_to_create=(out_dir.path,))
  process_result = yield process_request
  if process_result.exit_code:
    raise ...
  return ClasspathEntry(process_result.snapshot.re_root(outdir))
```

I expect that either as-part-of, or immediately-after @ity's current PR, we will want to do something like this. In the meantime though, continuing to have `input_conversion` and `output_conversion` functions will be ok: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/4d6427304aa5516a05398593c4d2ba86e44164ff/src/python/pants/engine/isolated_process.py#L150-L167

I saw that the Intrisic function ImportValue could do the job but what if the external resources are not managed through a cloudformation stack ?

An approach could be to define kind of RefArn attribute to be able to reference existing (static) ARN's of resources.
```
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Description: Simple CRUD webservice.
Resources:
  GetFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    RefArn: arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:xxxxxxxx:function:testLambda:current # this function already exists in AWS and I do not expect SAM to manage it
      Events:
        GetResource:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /resource/{resourceId}
            Method: get
```

Of course it has blocking drawbacks (SAM Local for instance), but is it a relevant feature regarding SAM objectives ?
Is their any workaround to have this working right now with SAM ?

Thanks for your feedback

issue_title: Large Table Creation Slow
issue_body:
I saw a closed issue you couldn't reproduce that sounded similar to what I'm seeing.  I was trying to create a table with 7 columns and around 1,000 rows and it took a very long time.  I was using a fork that had HTML tags so I wrote a test script, downloaded the most recent version of python-docx and I tried this on both ubuntu and windows machines running python2.7.

My test script is at the bottom, but all I did was create a document, add a 7 column table and then add blank rows.  Here is my time output on an i7 running python 2.7.9 and 64bit windows 7.  What I see is one core is pegged at 100% while this is running, but minimal memory usage.  You can see the line additions take longer and longer as the table gets bigger.

-Thanks

SCRIPT OUTPUT:
```

Total Runtime 134.46 seconds
```


SCRIPT CODE:
```
import time
import docx

STEP = 50
ROWS = 1000

print "docx Version: %s" % docx.__version__
document = docx.Document()
table = document.add_table(rows=1, cols=7)
tstart = time.time()
t1 = tstart
for i in range(ROWS):
    row_cells = table.add_row().cells
    if i and (i % STEP) == 0:
        t2 = time.time()
        print "%8.2fs:  %5d lines complete.  (%6.2f seconds for last %d lines)" % (t2 - tstart, i, t2-t1, STEP)
        t1 = t2

document.save("table_test.docx")
t2 = time.time()
print "Total Runtime %.2f seconds" % (t2 - tstart)
```
